blurb: Tall sword-leaved spikes unzip from bottom to top in mid-to-late summer, each carrying a column of big silky funnels in nearly every color — but the corms must be dug and stored frost-free each fall to survive a Front Range winter.
water: Moderate; keep evenly moist in summer, do not let dry out
spread: Clumping; each corm forms a new corm plus small cormels, but tender — does not naturalize or spread on the Front Range
seasons: Tall flower spikes mid-to-late summer (Jul–Sep); upright sword foliage through the growing season; nothing above or below ground in winter (corms are lifted and stored)
wildlife: The tubular flowers draw bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds; a useful late-summer nectar source
deer: Not deer-resistant — deer and rabbits will browse the foliage and flower stalks; protect where browsing pressure is high
toxic: Toxic — the corms (bulbs) are the most poisonous part, with leaves and roots also toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (drooling, vomiting, diarrhea); the sap can irritate skin

care.hardiness: The large florist gladiolus is NOT winter-hardy on the Front Range — it is reliable only to about USDA zone 7–8, so in Boulder's zone 5b–6a the corms freeze and die if left in the ground. Grow it as a tender bulb: plant in spring, lift in fall, and store the corms frost-free over winter (see Planting). The exception is the hardy dwarf types (e.g. Gladiolus nanus / 'Nanus' hybrids), which can sometimes overwinter in the ground in zones 5–6 with a deep, dry mulch in a well-drained, sheltered spot.[1][2][3]
care.planting: Plant corms in spring AFTER the danger of frost has passed — roughly mid-May around Boulder — once the soil has warmed; set them 3–6 in. deep and about 6 in. apart in full sun and well-drained soil. To LIFT for winter: when frost blackens the tops in fall (or about six weeks after flowering), dig the corms, cut the foliage to a short stub, let them cure/dry in a warm airy place for a couple of weeks, then twist off and discard the old shriveled corm beneath the new plump one.[5] Store the cleaned corms dry and frost-free in a ventilated box of peat, vermiculite, or sawdust at about 35–50°F (a cool basement or unheated-but-frost-free space) over winter, checking for rot; replant after the next spring's last frost.[1][4][5] For containers, treat them the same — plant after frost, then lift and store the corms or simply bring the pot somewhere frost-free, as the corms can't survive a Front Range winter outdoors.[1][2][3]
care.sun: Full sun — at least 6 hours — for strong, straight spikes and good flowering. In too much shade the spikes stretch, lean, and flower poorly.[3]
care.soil: Fertile, well-drained soil enriched with organic matter. Unlike many xeric bulbs, gladiolus wants soil that stays evenly moist (not waterlogged) through the growing season; it descends from summer-rainfall grassland. Loosen heavy clay with compost so it drains yet holds moisture.[3]
care.water: Moderate and steady through the growing season — keep the soil evenly moist while the plants are in active growth and bloom; do not let them dry out, especially as the flower spikes form, or bloom suffers. This is one of the thirstier 'bulbs' on the roster (not a xeric plant), so plan for regular summer watering on the Front Range. Ease off as the foliage yellows in fall before lifting.[3]
care.spacing: Space corms about 6 in. apart and 3–6 in. deep; planting a little deeper helps tall types stand without staking. For a cutting patch, plant in blocks or rows; mass plantings support each other against wind.[1][3]
care.propagation: By cormels (offsets): each season's corm produces a new corm plus a cluster of tiny cormels around its base — separate these at lifting, store them dry over winter, then plant the largest in spring; they reach blooming size in 1–2 seasons. By seed: possible for breeding but slow and won't come true for these hybrids — sow saved seed in spring and expect 2–3 years to first flower. Saving and growing on cormels is the practical way to increase a favorite.[1][3]
care.depth: Plant corms 3–6 in. deep (deeper in light soil and for tall varieties, to anchor the spikes), pointed growth-bud up. Store lifted corms dry, not buried, over winter.[1][3]
care.bloom: Mid-to-late summer — roughly July into September on the Front Range — about 70–100 days after planting depending on corm size and variety, each spike opening its funnels from the bottom upward.[3]
care.feeding: Feed for strong spikes: work a balanced fertilizer into the bed at planting and side-dress lightly when the flower spikes begin to show. Don't overdo nitrogen, which favors leaves over flowers.[3]
care.maintenance: Stake or corral tall varieties — the heavy one-sided spikes flop in wind and rain; planting deeper and in blocks reduces the need. Stagger plantings every two weeks from mid-May into June for a succession of bloom through the summer. Cut spikes for the vase when the lowest one or two florets open. After bloom, leave foliage to feed the corm until frost, then lift, cure, and store (see Planting).[1][4][5]
care.troubles: The biggest 'failure' is leaving corms in the ground over winter — they freeze and die; lift and store them. Thrips are the main pest, streaking and distorting leaves and flowers (inspect and treat stored corms); botrytis and corm rot strike in wet, poorly drained soil or damp storage — store dry and ventilated. Floppy spikes are a staking/depth issue.[1][3]
care.companions: Pair with other full-sun summer bloomers that enjoy regular water — dahlias (also lifted and stored), zinnias, and summer annuals make a classic cutting-garden combination; lower bushy plants in front help screen the bare gladiolus stems and offer some wind support.[2][3]

edible.summary: Gladiolus is toxic and not a food plant — the corms (bulbs) are the most poisonous part, with leaves and roots also toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; the sap can irritate skin.[2][3]
edible.caution: All parts of Gladiolus × hortulanus are toxic, with the toxin most concentrated in the underground corms (bulbs). Ingestion by dogs, cats, or horses causes salivation/drooling, vomiting, lethargy, and diarrhea; severity is generally low for a small accidental nibble, but the plant is not edible and should be kept away from pets and livestock. Handling the corms and sap can cause skin irritation in sensitive people — wear gloves when dividing and cleaning corms. Note: while the petals of some garden gladiolus are sometimes used as a garnish with the bitter anthers removed, this guide treats the plant as non-food given the toxic corms/foliage and the risk of misidentification — do not eat the corms, leaves, or roots. If a pet ingests any part, call ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) or your veterinarian; for people, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).[2][3]
